shortDescription.text: NoSQL injection via $where clause with string interpolation of user input

fullDescription.text: The getByUserIdAndThreshold function constructs a MongoDB $where clause using template literal string interpolation with the threshold parameter sourced from req.query.threshold. The $where operator executes arbitrary JavaScript on the MongoDB server. An attacker can inject JavaScript code via the threshold parameter to bypass query logic (e.g., `1'; return 1 == '1`) or cause denial of service (e.g., `0';while(true){}'`). This is a critical server-side JavaScript injection vulnerability.

shortDescription.text: Server-side JavaScript injection via MongoDB $where operator

fullDescription.text: The $where clause in MongoDB evaluates JavaScript expressions on the server. By injecting into the threshold parameter, an attacker achieves arbitrary server-side JavaScript execution within the MongoDB context. This can be used to exfiltrate data from other collections, manipulate query results, or perform denial-of-service attacks via infinite loops.

shortDescription.text: Denial of service via infinite loop injection in $where clause

fullDescription.text: An attacker can inject an infinite loop (e.g., `0';while(true){}'`) into the threshold parameter. Because the $where clause executes JavaScript on the MongoDB server, this blocks the database thread, causing denial of service for all database operations. MongoDB's $where does not enforce execution time limits by default.

shortDescription.text: Upsert flag enables unintended document creation

fullDescription.text: The update method in allocations-dao.js uses {upsert: true}, which creates a new document if no matching document exists. If an attacker manipulates the userId or other query parameters, this could create unexpected allocation records in the database, violating data integrity constraints and potentially enabling privilege escalation through fabricated financial data.

message.text: The getByUserIdAndThreshold function builds a MongoDB $where clause via string interpolation: `$where: \`this.userId == ${parsedUserId} && this.stocks > '${threshold}'\``. The threshold parameter originates from req.query.threshold with no sanitization, enabling NoSQL injection. An attacker can inject `1'; return 1 == '1` to bypass the filter and retrieve all allocation records.

The $where operator in getByUserIdAndThreshold executes a JavaScript expression on the MongoDB server. Because the threshold value is interpolated directly into this expression without escaping, an attacker gains server-side JavaScript execution within the MongoDB process. This allows reading data from arbitrary collections, manipulating results, or executing privileged database operations.

An attacker can cause denial of service by injecting `0';while(true){}'` as the threshold parameter. This creates an infinite loop in the MongoDB $where JavaScript evaluation, blocking the database thread. No input validation or query timeout is configured to prevent this attack.

The update method uses {upsert: true}, which will create a new allocation document if no match is found. Combined with the lack of input validation on query parameters, an attacker could create fabricated financial allocation records, compromising data integrity in the allocations collection.
